region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; widely consumed in Singapore as part of the Indian community's culinary heritage

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for khichree; 'rice dish' (FOODON_03311069) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for lentil-rice preparations is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GG (Rice-based dishes) is the closest defensible category. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for khichree or lentil-rice composite dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for khichree or equivalent lentil-rice preparation as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of khichree is approximately 200 g (one bowl), providing around 238 kcal, 11.6 g protein, 35.2 g carbohydrate, and 6.8 g dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: Khichree — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Khichree (Indian-style rice cooked with split mung bean, lentil, pea, onion, ginger, chilli and spices). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~1.0 g/100g based on the ghee/oil component typical of this preparation; this field is marked as estimated. Potassium estimated at ~220 mg/100g consistent with the lentil-rice composition and referenced in the input health_suitability notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for lentil-rice composite dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for khichree in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on analogous lentil-rice composite dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database, where rice-lentil mixtures (dal-rice, kitchari-type preparations) consistently yield GI values in the 48–55 range. The lentil component (split mung bean, masoor dal) is well-established as a GI-lowering ingredient when combined with white rice, reducing the blended GI from ~72 (plain white rice) to approximately 48–55. GL per 200 g serving = (50 × 35.2) / 100 = 17.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium at 178 mg/100g with meaningful potassium (~220 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g) supporting DASH dietary pattern
hypertension.guidance: Khichree is suitable for individuals with hypertension when prepared without added salt; the lentil-rice combination provides potassium and fibre that support blood pressure regulation, but high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papadums should be avoided to keep the meal's total sodium within DASH targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~50) from lentil component, dietary fibre 3.4 g/100g slowing glucose absorption, moderate carbohydrate at 17.6 g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Khichree has a low estimated GI of approximately 50 due to the lentil and mung bean content, making it a more glycaemically favourable choice than plain white rice; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 150–200 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables to further reduce glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.0 g/100g), high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g) from lentils, plant-based protein, anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Khichree is a heart-friendly dish with low saturated fat, meaningful dietary fibre from lentils that supports LDL cholesterol reduction, and anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric and ginger; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prepare it with minimal ghee and avoid high-sodium accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Curcumin (turmeric) and gingerol (ginger) are evidence-based anti-inflammatory compounds; low saturated fat; plant protein without arachidonic acid burden
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Khichree is well-suited for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric and ginger in the spice base; maximising turmeric and ginger quantities and substituting ghee with extra-virgin olive oil can further enhance anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Khichree (also spelled Khichdi, Khichri, or Kitchari) is a traditional Indian one-pot dish made by slow-cooking white rice together with split mung beans, lentils (masoor dal), green peas, onion, ginger, chilli, and a blend of warming spices including turmeric, cumin, and coriander, typically finished with ghee or oil. The result is a soft, porridge-like dish with a mild, earthy, and gently spiced flavour profile and a creamy, cohesive texture. It is one of the oldest recorded foods in the Indian subcontinent and is widely consumed across India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and within the Indian diaspora communities of Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, khichree provides 119 kcal per 100 g, 5.8 g protein, 2.8 g fat, 17.6 g carbohydrate, and 3.4 g dietary fibre. The lentil and mung bean content significantly lowers the glycaemic index compared to plain white rice, making it a more suitable carbohydrate source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Dietary fibre at 3.4 g/100g supports gut health and cholesterol management. Turmeric (curcumin) and ginger (gingerol) in the spice base are clinically recognised anti-inflammatory compounds relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease management. Sodium is moderate at 178 mg/100g. Khichree is naturally gluten-free, vegetarian, and halal. It is commonly served as a complete meal, as a recovery food during illness, or as a weaning food for infants due to its digestibility and balanced macronutrient profile.
common_user_queries: Indian rice and lentil dish nutrition

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match to this dish. Saturated fat and potassium are estimated from compositional analysis of the known ingredients and are documented as estimated. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for lentil-rice composite dishes, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.0 g/100g) is estimated based on the ghee/oil component typical of khichree preparation; this value is not directly reported in the HPB source and is marked as an approximation. 2. Potassium (220 mg/100g) is estimated from the lentil-rice composition; not directly reported in the HPB source. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this specific khichree preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 4. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database lentil-rice composite entries; no direct GI measurement for khichree exists in the database. 5. is_vegan set to false as ghee (clarified butter) is a traditional ingredient; a vegan variant using oil would qualify. 6. is_dairy_free set to false for the same reason. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology term for khichree exists in current releases. 8. GL per serving calculated as (50 × 35.2 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 17.6.
